payload.summary: All four amendments identify real defects or missing coverage in the timeout and process-management changes: timeout cleanup is bypassed by the service wrapper, timeout artifact preservation is not tested, non-Unix timeout handling can leak child processes, and exit code 127 is being misclassified as a missing backend binary after successful spawn.

votes.rationale: `src/contexts/agent_execution/service.rs` starts an outer `tokio::time::timeout(request.timeout, ...)` around `adapter.invoke()`, while `src/adapters/process_backend.rs` starts another timeout with the same duration later inside `spawn_and_wait()`. Because the outer timer starts first, the inner timeout path that calls `preserve_failed_artifacts()` is effectively preempted in normal service use. The service timeout only calls `cancel()`, which does not preserve Codex temp artifacts, so timeout diagnostics can be lost.

The existing timeout test in `tests/unit/process_backend_test.rs` only checks that the error is classified as `FailureClass::Timeout`. It does not verify the observable cleanup side effect under `runtime/failed`, so a regression in artifact preservation would pass unnoticed. The exact assertions may need to vary by backend, but the coverage gap described by the amendment is real and worth fixing.

On non-Unix, `send_sigkill()` and `send_sigterm()` return `Unsupported` when the process still exists. `spawn_background_reap()` ignores the `send_sigkill()` failure and then awaits `child.wait()` forever, which keeps ownership of `ManagedChild` alive and prevents `kill_on_drop(true)` from helping. In the service timeout path, `cancel()` can also fail early on non-Unix and that error is ignored by the caller. The result is a genuine timeout-with-live-child leak on Windows.

Actual missing executables are already detected before or at spawn time through `resolve_binary()` and `command.spawn()` returning `ErrorKind::NotFound`. After a child has successfully spawned, exit status `127` is not reliable evidence that the backend binary itself was missing; it is just process-defined exit behavior, especially for wrapper scripts or CLIs that invoke subcommands. Classifying every post-spawn `127` as `BinaryNotFound` incorrectly turns a retryable process failure into a non-retryable configuration error.
